skill.name: azure-servicebus-ts
skill.description: Build TypeScript messaging workflows with the Azure Service Bus SDK, including queues, topics, sessions, scheduled messages, and dead-letter handling.
skill.summary: Create Azure Service Bus queue and topic workflows in TypeScript.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, weak cryptography, environment access, and reconnaissance patterns. Manual review found the command and weak-crypto detections are false positives from markdown fences, prose, and SDK examples; the only real risk factor is a normal process.env namespace lookup in sample TypeScript.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access in Example Code
low_findings.description: The skill shows process.env.SERVICEBUS_NAMESPACE in a TypeScript authentication example. This is normal configuration usage and no secret exfiltration is shown, but users should avoid placing secrets in prompts or logs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 32 directly reads one environment variable in sample code. The value is a Service Bus namespace, and no code sends environment data to an untrusted destination.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Detections Are Markdown Fences
low_findings.description: The external command findings correspond to fenced markdown examples and SDK sample snippets, not executable shell or Ruby backtick operations. The only shell command shown is an installation example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are markdown code fences or examples, not hidden execution logic. No instruction asks the agent to run untrusted commands beyond a package installation command.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Detections Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits align with normal words, headings, and Service Bus methods such as dead-letter descriptions, non-destructive peek, receive modes, and reference link titles. The reconnaissance hit is best-practice prose about Entra ID authentication.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain documentation text and Azure Service Bus terminology. No cryptographic algorithm, system enumeration command, or host reconnaissance behavior is present.

content.value_statement: Messaging failures and queue design choices can slow TypeScript services. This skill gives focused Azure Service Bus patterns for queues, topics, sessions, dead letters, scheduling, and settlement.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and import the Azure Service Bus and Azure Identity SDK packages.
Demonstrates DefaultAzureCredential authentication with a Service Bus namespace.
Provides queue send, batch send, receive, and subscription receiver examples.
Covers topics, subscriptions, sessions, scheduled messages, and message deferral.
Explains dead-letter handling, peek behavior, and receive mode choices.
limitations: Does not provision Azure Service Bus namespaces, queues, topics, or subscriptions.
Does not verify Azure credentials, permissions, or tenant configuration.
Examples are focused snippets, not complete production services.
Does not include performance testing or cost optimization guidance.

use_cases.title: Add Queue Processing to a Service
use_cases.description: Use the skill to draft sender and receiver flows for a TypeScript backend that processes asynchronous work.

use_cases.title: Standardize Messaging Reliability
use_cases.description: Use the skill to document settlement, dead-letter, error handling, and resource cleanup expectations.

use_cases.title: Model Ordered Enterprise Workflows
use_cases.description: Use the skill to choose topics, subscriptions, sessions, and scheduling for integration workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Send a Queue Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-servicebus-ts skill to outline a TypeScript sender for one queue message using DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Add Topic Publishing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-servicebus-ts skill to design a topic publisher and subscription receiver for order events.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Session Processing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-servicebus-ts skill to explain how message sessions preserve order for a workflow with multiple steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Reliable Consumer
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-servicebus-ts skill to review a Service Bus consumer plan for settlement, dead letters, receive modes, scheduling, and cleanup.

output_examples.input: Create a basic queue sender for a TypeScript service.
output_examples.output: A concise implementation plan covering authentication, sender creation, message shape, send flow, and resource cleanup.

output_examples.input: Explain how to process failed messages.
output_examples.output: A dead-letter handling summary with failure reasons, separate dead-letter receivers, reprocessing choices, and completion guidance.

output_examples.input: Choose between sessions and topics for an order workflow.
output_examples.output: A comparison of ordered session processing and pub-sub fanout, with recommendations based on message grouping and subscribers.

best_practices: Use Entra ID authentication for production workloads instead of connection strings.
Reuse Service Bus clients and close senders or receivers when work is complete.
Handle errors and dead-letter queues as part of every consumer design.
anti_patterns: Do not use receive-and-delete mode for messages that require reliable processing.
Do not ignore processError callbacks in subscription receivers.
Do not create a new client for every message operation.

faq.question: Does this skill create Azure resources?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on TypeScript SDK usage after Service Bus resources already exist.

faq.question: Can it help with queues and topics?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for queue senders, queue receivers, topic publishing, and subscription receiving.

faq.question: Does it support message sessions?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows session IDs, session receivers, and session state patterns.

faq.question: Does it cover dead-letter queues?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains moving messages to dead letter and reading from the dead-letter subqueue.

faq.question: Is it specific to TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples use the Azure Service Bus SDK for JavaScript with TypeScript syntax.

faq.question: Should I put secrets in prompts?
faq.answer: No. Use placeholders for namespaces, queue names, and credentials when asking for help.
